caveat: Figures are official data from the Office for National Statistics (ONS) and the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR), the latest available for each measure. Public finance figures are provisional and routinely revised. Monthly borrowing is seasonal and should not be multiplied up to estimate a year.

metrics.metric_name: Public sector net debt (excluding public sector banks)
metrics.explanation: The headline measure of how much the UK government owes, after subtracting liquid assets. This is the main measure used in the monthly public finance statistics.
metrics.whatThisMeans: Think of it as everything the government has borrowed and not yet repaid, less the cash it holds. It is a stock, a total built up over many years, not a single year's spending. As a share of GDP this is around levels last seen in the early 1960s.
metrics.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: June 2026
metrics.notes: £2,989.9bn at end June 2026, the latest published cash figure, £122.3bn more than a year earlier. Excludes public sector banks. Monthly cash figures are revised routinely: end-May was revised down £8.3bn in this release.

metrics.metric_name: Debt as a share of GDP
metrics.explanation: Net debt measured against the size of the economy for that period. This matters more than the cash total, because a bigger economy can support more debt.
metrics.whatThisMeans: If debt is around 95% of GDP, the government owes a little less than the economy produces in a year. Economists watch the trend in this ratio rather than the cash figure alone.
metrics.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: June 2026
metrics.notes: 94.9% of GDP at end June 2026, provisionally estimated, 0.4 percentage points higher than a year earlier.

metrics.explanation: Total net debt divided by the UK population. A way to picture the scale, not a bill anyone personally owes.
metrics.whatThisMeans: This does not mean each person owes this amount. Government debt is repaid through taxes and borrowing over time, not split between residents.
metrics.source_name: Calculated from ONS net debt and ONS population estimate
metrics.notes: Calculated at build time: net debt (£2,990bn) divided by population estimate (69.5m). Recomputed whenever either input changes.

metrics.explanation: What the government pays to service its debt over a year. Affected both by how much is owed and by the interest rate on it.
metrics.whatThisMeans: Interest is a real cash cost competing with other spending, around 8% of total public spending. Some of it rises and falls with inflation, because a share of UK debt is index-linked. Central government interest payable was £11.8 billion in June 2026 alone.
metrics.source_name: OBR, Economic and Fiscal Outlook March 2026 (central government debt interest, net of APF)
metrics.notes: £109.7bn in 2025-26 (OBR March 2026 forecast), 3.6% of GDP on the OBR's own basis. Includes the inflation uplift on index-linked gilts. The ONS outturn measure of central government net interest payable is narrower and lower, at £96.9bn for the same year.

metrics.explanation: How much the government borrowed in the latest month, the gap between what it spent and what it raised.
metrics.whatThisMeans: Borrowing is a flow (one period's gap), while debt is the stock that builds up over many periods. Monthly borrowing is seasonal (April is typically high), so it cannot be multiplied by 12 to get the year. Annual borrowing is around £128 billion.
metrics.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: June 2026
metrics.notes: £16.0bn in June 2026, the seventh-highest June on record and £0.3bn below the OBR forecast of £16.3bn. Borrowing in the financial year to June was £57.6bn, £2.7bn above forecast. Excludes public sector banks.

gdp.notes: The nominal GDP consistent with the published debt-to-GDP ratio (net debt 2990 / 0.949). Calendar-year 2025 nominal GDP outturn is about £3,034bn; this measure is used for the translator's share-of-GDP figure.

adults.notes: Population aged 18 and over, UK mid-2024 estimate, the latest published age breakdown. ONS publishes the mid-2025 age breakdown from 29 July 2026 (England and Wales first), so this sits one vintage behind the population figure above.

taxpayers.notes: Number of income tax payers (HMRC, tax year 2023-24 outturn, published 15 July 2026). Later years are projections and run several million higher.

taxReceipts.notes: Central government total current receipts, financial year ending March 2026 (ONS). This is not the same as total tax receipts, which are lower at £1,108bn on a National Accounts basis (OBR March 2026), because it includes interest, dividends and other non-tax income. Total public sector current receipts are higher, around £1,235bn.
